banner.noActivity: Your AI assistant has not been used yet {{period}}. Once you connect an AI client to a server, its activity will appear here.
banner.allGood_one: Everything is running smoothly. Your AI completed all {{total}} task successfully {{period}}.
banner.allGood_other: Everything is running smoothly. Your AI completed all {{total}} tasks successfully {{period}}.
banner.someErrors_one: Your AI ran {{total}} task {{period}}. {{successes}} went well — {{errors}} had issues. Check the projects below for details.
banner.someErrors_other: Your AI ran {{total}} tasks {{period}}. {{successes}} went well — {{errors}} had issues. Check the projects below for details.
banner.manyErrors_one: Attention needed. {{errors}} out of {{total}} task had problems {{period}}. Please review your server settings.
banner.manyErrors_other: Attention needed. {{errors}} out of {{total}} tasks had problems {{period}}. Please review your server settings.
banner.noServers: You have no servers yet. Create your first server to start connecting your AI assistant to an API.

help.overviewIntro: This page shows you what your AI assistant has been doing — how many tasks it ran, whether they succeeded, and which integrations are healthy right now. It is your single source of truth for understanding whether your AI is working correctly and how much it is being used.
help.overviewReadability: You do not need any technical knowledge to read this page. The summary banner at the top translates raw numbers into plain sentences — 'Everything is working well' or 'X tasks had issues' — so you always know the current situation at a glance.
help.overviewPeriod: Use the period selector in the top-right to switch between today, this week, this month, or a custom date range. All numbers, charts, and the status banner update instantly. If you see a spike in errors on the chart, narrow the period to isolate when it started.
help.overviewTroubleshooting: Select the expected period and use Refresh. No activity can be valid, but if calls should be present, confirm that the AI client is connected and open the server's Activity tab. If the page cannot load, check your permission to view servers and try again.
help.tasksTitle: How many tasks your AI ran
help.tasksContent: Each time your AI assistant calls one of your integrations — for example 'look up this customer' or 'create a new ticket' — that counts as one task. The number here shows the total for the selected period. A high count with a high success rate means your AI is working well. A high count with a low success rate means something is misconfigured and needs attention — open the affected server to see its Activity Log.
help.tasksSuccess: The total changes when you change the period, and the succeeded count matches the completed requests shown below.
help.tasksWhenFail: If you see a red or yellow subline, open the server mentioned in the summary and check its Activity Log for the specific error messages. Most failures are caused by expired API credentials, wrong endpoint paths, or an upstream service being temporarily down.
help.integrationsContent: Each integration is one server — a connection from Arthur to one external API such as a CRM, ticketing system, or database. The number shown is your total server count. The subline tells you how many are currently active and running without errors. A server that is paused or misconfigured does not count as 'running normally'.
help.integrationsSuccess: The total matches the Servers page, and servers expected to receive calls appear active rather than paused.
help.integrationsTip: If the active count seems low, check the Servers page for any server showing a red dot or a Paused chip. A server with zero tools also serves no useful purpose — make sure each server has at least one tool configured before connecting it to an AI client.
help.actionsContent: Each action is one enabled tool across all your servers — something specific your AI can do, like 'search for a contact', 'create an order', or 'get server health'. The total here is the sum of all enabled tools across all active servers. More actions means your AI can help with a wider range of tasks. However, many tools with similar names or vague descriptions can confuse the AI — give each tool a unique, specific name.
help.actionsSuccess: Open a server's Tools section and confirm that the tools the AI should use are enabled and have clear names and descriptions.
help.actionsTip: If this number is zero, your AI has nothing it can do yet — go to a server and add at least one tool, either manually or by importing an OpenAPI spec. If the number seems lower than expected, some tools may be disabled — disabled tools are hidden from the AI but still visible in the server's tool list.
help.securityContent: This card counts servers that have at least one MCP Access Key. An Access Key protects the MCP endpoint; it is separate from the credential Arthur uses to call the source API. Protect servers that expose production or sensitive data.
help.securityHowTo: Open the server's Connect section, create an Access Key, and configure that key in the AI client using one of the formats shown there.
help.securitySuccess: The secured count increases and the AI client can connect with the key, while a request without valid authentication is rejected.
help.securityTip: If connection fails, copy the key again and check whether the client sends it in the supported header or query parameter. Revoke and replace keys that may have been exposed. A server can also use OAuth, which is configured separately in Connect and is not included in this Access Key count.
help.chartHint: Each bar represents one time slot in the selected period — hours for 'Today', days for 'This week' or 'This month'. Hover over any bar to see the exact request count and how many failed. Use this to spot patterns: a daily spike suggests a scheduled job; a sudden drop to zero may mean connectivity was lost.
help.chartBlue: All requests in this slot succeeded — the integration was healthy.
help.chartYellow: Some requests in this slot had issues — a partial outage or intermittent errors. Check the Activity Log of the affected server to understand the cause.
help.chartRed: All requests in this slot failed — the integration was completely unavailable. Likely caused by an outage, expired credentials, or a configuration change.
help.chartSuccess: Hover over a bar and confirm that its call and failure counts agree with the selected period and the task summary.
help.chartTroubleshooting: An empty chart means no calls were recorded for that period. If calls were expected, use Refresh, confirm the date range, then check the AI client's connection and the server's Activity tab.
help.topActionsContent: The tools your AI called most often in the selected period, ranked by call count. These are your most critical actions — they are where a misconfiguration or downtime will have the biggest impact. Make sure top actions have accurate descriptions and reliable upstream API connections. If an expected action is missing, check whether it is enabled and whether its description clearly communicates its purpose to the AI.
help.topActionsSuccess: The expected tools appear in the ranking after the AI client calls them, and each row shows the server that owns the tool.
help.topActionsTroubleshooting: No entries means no tool calls were recorded in the selected period. If the wrong tool is used, improve tool names and descriptions; if a tool is missing, confirm that it is enabled and visible to the connected client.
help.statusHint: Shows the current health of each server based on the last hour of activity. Unlike the chart above which shows history, this reflects what is happening right now.
help.statusGreen: All requests in the last hour succeeded. The server is healthy.
help.statusYellow: Some requests in the last hour had issues (1–19% failure rate). The server is partially degraded — check its Activity Log before the situation worsens.
help.statusRed: Many requests in the last hour are failing (20%+ failure rate). This server needs immediate attention — check its Activity Log and authentication configuration.
help.statusGrey: No activity in the last hour, or the server is paused. A grey dot is expected for a server that is not being actively used. If you expected activity here, check whether the AI client is still connected.
help.statusSuccess: Servers receiving successful calls show green. Click a server row to open it and compare the status with its recent Activity entries.
help.statusTroubleshooting: Yellow or red calls for checking the source service, credentials, and recent errors. Grey is not necessarily a failure: it can mean no recent calls or a paused server.

tasks.title: How did tasks go?
tasks.noActivity: No activity yet in this period.
tasks.issueHint_one: 1 task had an issue. Open the relevant server and check its logs to see what went wrong.
tasks.issueHint_other: {{count}} tasks had issues. Open the affected servers and check their logs to find out what went wrong.
